data.title: U.S. Defense Stocks Are Recovering, European Ones Are Soaring. How Trump Changed the Outlook. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Brian Swint

Defense stocks continued their meager comeback early Wednesday after President Donald Trump's recent promise to slash military spending. European defense firms are still outpacing them by some distance.

Big contractors Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, and L3Harris have all lost more than 10% over the past month. They were rising 0.6%, 0.9%, and 0.1%, respectively, before the market opened. The exception is Palantir, the artificial intelligence darling that benefits from defense spending, which was falling 0.8% in the premarket--though it has gained 62% in the month on AI optimism.

It's a very different story in Europe, though. The Trump administration's rhetoric about pulling back from overseas military commitments, spending less on defense, and engagement with Russia to end the war in Ukraine has created a sense of crisis across the Atlantic. Investors expect countries to ramp up spending on fighter jets, tanks, and other equipment, bolstering the shares of the domestic companies that can build it.

Germany's Rheinmetall has been one of the biggest beneficiaries. It was rising 1.5% on Wednesday and has gained more than 35% in the past month. Thales, a French contractor that has some similarities with Palantir, was up 1.3%, and its one-month advance is greater than 20%. The U.K.'s BAE Systems was up 0.9% on the day after posting earnings Wednesday, and it's 10% higher than it was a month ago.

Of course, American companies may also get orders from Europe for new equipment, but probably not as many as firms closer to home. Traditionally, U.S. military spending has far outstripped its allies. Germany and France finally managed to get spending up to 2% of gross domestic product last year, the target set by the North Atlantic Treaty Organization after Russia annexed Crimea in 2014.

NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte said last week that the spending target may need to rise even higher.

Write to Brian Swint at brian.swint@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Parsons Reiterates Long-term Growth Targets >PSN
OGE Energy's Q4 Net Income, Revenue Rise; Provides Full-Year Outlook
data.description:
OGE Energy reported Q4 net income Wednesday of $0.50 per diluted share, up from $0.24 a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected $0.49.

Revenue for the quarter ended Dec. 31 was $760.5 million, up from $566.7 million a year earlier.

Two analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $750.8 million.

For 2025, the company expects consolidated earnings of $2.21 to $2.33 per average diluted share. Analysts polled by FactSet expect $2.28.

OGE Energy maintained a second-quarter dividend of $0.42125 per share, payable on April 25 to shareholders of record on April 7.

data.title: Cinemark Reinstates Dividend >CNK
Parsons Corporation Q4 and Fiscal Year 2024 Financial Results
data.description:
Parsons Corporation has released its financial results for the fourth quarter and fiscal year ending December 31, 2024. The company has reported record-breaking figures since its IPO, showcasing significant growth across various financial metrics.

Financial Highlights

For the fourth quarter of 2024, Parsons reported a record revenue of $1.7 billion, marking a 16% increase year-over-year. The company achieved an organic revenue growth of 14%, continuing its streak of double-digit organic growth for the eighth consecutive quarter. Net income for the quarter rose by 21% to $54 million, while adjusted EBITDA increased by 14% to $147 million. Cash flow from operations was reported at $127 million.

For the fiscal year 2024, Parsons reported a record revenue of $6.8 billion, representing a 24% year-over-year growth with an organic growth of 22%. Net income surged by 46% to $235 million, and adjusted EBITDA increased by 30% to $605 million. The company also reported a record cash flow from operations, which increased by 28% to $524 million.

Business and Operational Highlights

Parsons' Federal Solutions segment saw a 19% increase in revenue for Q4 2024, driven by organic growth and contributions from acquisitions. The Critical Infrastructure segment also reported a 12% increase in revenue for the same period. For the fiscal year, Federal Solutions revenue increased by 33%, while Critical Infrastructure revenue grew by 13%.

Significant contract wins included two new contracts in Saudi Arabia totaling over $275 million, a $242 million option year contract with a confidential customer, and a lead design contract for the Newark AirTrain Replacement Program. The company also secured a $122 million option period contract with the Department of State and a $104 million option year on its General Services Administration C5ISR contract.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

During the quarter, Parsons announced and closed the acquisition of BCC Engineering for $230 million and TRS Group for $36 million. These acquisitions are expected to strengthen Parsons' position in infrastructure and environmental remediation. The company was also recognized as one of America’s most trusted companies by Forbes and received several other accolades, including being named "Contractor of the Year" at the Greater Washington Government Contractor Awards.

Management's Perspective

Carey Smith, chair, president, and CEO of Parsons, commented on the exceptional year, highlighting the company's record results in revenue, adjusted EBITDA, operating cash flow, and contract awards. Smith emphasized the company's balanced portfolio and strong execution in its six large and growing end-markets, which have enabled Parsons to capitalize on global infrastructure spending and federal opportunities.

Future Outlook

Parsons has provided its fiscal year 2025 guidance, projecting revenue between $7.0 billion and $7.5 billion, adjusted EBITDA between $640 million and $710 million, and cash flow from operating activities between $420 million and $480 million. The company also reiterated its long-term growth targets, aiming for mid-single-digit or better organic revenue growth and continual margin improvement.

SEC Filing: PARSONS CORP [ PSN ] - 8-K - Feb. 19, 2025

UK Market Talk Roundup: Brokers Comments
data.description:
Broker comments in the UK today. Compiled by Dow Jones Newswires Markets Desk, markets.eu@dowjones.com

1123 GMT - Glencore's "controversial" decision to retain its coal assets was a drag on its full-year results as coal prices weakened in 2024, head of investment analysis at AJ Bell, Laith Khalaf, writes. The decision to stick with coal raised eyebrows among investors and the miner needs to make this look like a smart business decision sooner rather than later, Khalaf adds. Glencore is also considering shifting its primary listing in an attempt to get a better valuation. "The fact the company's profitability is dictated by volatile commodity prices, over which it has no control, is always likely to be an obstacle to a really premium valuation," he writes. Shares trade down 7% at 328.55 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1024 GMT - Jet2 could face pretax profit consensus downgrades of around 2% to 3% after it warned that cost headwinds and pressure on consumer incomes could weigh on profitability in its next fiscal year, RBC Capital Markets' Ruairi Cullinane writes. Given the flagged headwinds for fiscal 2026 pretax profit, the consensus could come in at 575 million pounds to 580 million pounds, the analyst says in a note. The London-listed leisure-travel group is facing input cost pressures exceeding headline consumer price index increases for hotel accommodation, aircraft maintenance and general airport and Eurocontrol charges, Cullinane says. Shares trade down 10% to 14.02 pounds. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0959 GMT - Glencore's 2024 shareholder returns were a little behind consensus expectations, Barclays's analysts wrote in a research note. The commodities giant said it would return $2.2 billion to shareholders. This compares with consensus estimates of around $2.33 billion, analysts say. However, Barclays had expected $1.4 billion. Glencore will face near-term headwinds given lower coal prices but the medium-term outlook is positive, they write. Shareholder returns should move higher over the medium term, they write. Shares fall 6.3% to 331.10 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0926 GMT - BAE Systems released a strong set of 2024 results, with outperforming free cash flow due to high cash conversion in the Platforms and Services, and Air divisions, Berenberg analysts say. However, the London-listed aerospace and defense group's free cash flow guidance for 2025 is about 700 million pounds below consensus, they note. The implied earnings per share guidance for this year is 1% below consensus, they add. Still, the analysts believe Wednesday's results should be taken positively. Shares are down 2.4% at 13.05 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0843 GMT - Shares in easyJet and TUI fall, dragged by leisure-travel group peer Jet2's warning that its profit margin could come under pressure. easyJet shares in London trade down 2.9% at 502.60 pence while shares for German travel company TUI are 4% lower at 6.81 euros. Jet2 shares are down 9.65% at 14.14 pounds. Jet2 said that although bookings are up on-year for April, May and June, current macro-economic conditions, pressure on consumer incomes and customers' preference for booking late risk weighing on its performance. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0828 GMT - Glencore switching its listing to the U.S. would be a positive catalyst for its shares, Jefferies analysts Christopher LaFemina and Patricia Hove write. The Anglo-Swiss miner said Wednesday that it is reviewing its primary-listing status. Glencore's $1 billion share buyback also helps ease concerns over the slight miss to 2024 earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization expectations, they write. Meanwhile, Glencore is not expected to benefit from strong commodity tailwinds until next year, they write. Shares trade down 3% at 342.7 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0753 GMT - BAE Systems' 2024 results beat market expectations, J.P. Morgan analyst David Perry says. The British aerospace and defense group reported earnings per share 3% ahead of Vuma consensus, while its free cash flow was 890 million pounds above consensus, he says. The company's evolution in 2026 should become clearer in the coming months, once the U.S. administration tables its 2026 defense budget in April, and the U.K. government sets out its military spending plans, Perry notes. "We recommend the shares as a long-term holding; however, after an 11% rally in the last five days we don't expect a major reaction today," he adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0733 GMT - The FTSE 100 is seen opening little changed, according to IG. The index closed flat to 8766 points on Tuesday. Data Wednesday showed U.K. inflation increased to 3.0% year-on-year in January from 2.5% in December. Economists in a WSJ survey expected 2.8%. Core inflation rose to 3.7% from 3.2% a month ago, as expected. The Bank of England's "gradual and careful" pace of cutting interest rates looks set to continue for the foreseeable future, Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. While more rapid rate cuts are possible in the second half amid stalling economic momentum, stubbornly high inflation won't permit this yet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0711 GMT - HSBC's medium-term guidance for its return on tangible equity will likely be a focus in management's call with analysts and investors, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note after the bank's results. The assumptions underpinning the guidance will prompt questions, analysts Benjamin Toms and Pablo de la Torre Cuevas say. HSBC kept its midterm ROTE guidance unchanged in the mid-teens excluding notable items, while consensus pencils in a 14% return. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0704 GMT - HSBC produced a respectable set of fourth-quarter numbers, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note. The bank's adjusted pretax profit beat consensus on better income and costs, which were partially offset by higher impairments, analysts Elise Yu Ge and Edward Firth say. Its $2 billion share buyback feels light against its recent $3 billion run rate but the dividend it proposed was slightly better, they write. HSBC is the most expensive U.K. bank on a price-to-earnings basis, which is difficult to justify for a group delivering little loan growth and targeting mid-teens returns, the analysts say. "Some may argue that HSBC has traditionally traded at a premium to U.K. banks, but to suggest that Asia/Hong Kong is the same opportunity as it was 15 year ago is obviously flawed," they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

Contact us in London. +44-20-7842-9288
Markettalk.eu@dowjones.com
